section.title: The Greatest Commandment
section.sectionRationale: This chapter holds the Shema, the confession and command that Jesus names as the greatest commandment in the whole law, making it the theological center not only of Deuteronomy's statutes but of Israel's entire relationship with God.
section.thesis: At the heart of Moses' address stands the Shema: the LORD is one, and Israel must love Him with all their heart, soul, and strength, teaching these words continually to their children so that prosperity in the land never displaces wholehearted devotion to Him.
section.frameNote: Five movements: the purpose of the commandments; the Shema itself, hear, love, and teach; the warning against forgetting God in prosperity; the warning against testing the LORD; and teaching the next generation why these statutes matter.

units.label: The purpose of the commandments
units.note: so that you may fear the LORD

verses.text: These are the commandments and statutes and ordinances that the LORD your God has instructed me to teach you to follow in the land that you are about to enter and possess,

so that you and your children and grandchildren may fear the LORD your God all the days of your lives by keeping all His statutes and commandments that I give you, and so that your days may be prolonged.
Hear, O Israel, and be careful to observe them, so that you may prosper and multiply greatly in a land flowing with milk and honey, just as the LORD, the God of your fathers, has promised you.

units.label: The Shema: hear, love, and teach
units.note: with all your heart, soul, and strength

verses.text: {{shema|Hear}}, O Israel: The LORD our God, the LORD is {{echad|One}}.
And you shall {{ahav|love}} the LORD your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your strength.
These words I am commanding you today are to be upon your hearts.
And you shall teach them diligently to your children and speak of them when you sit at home and when you walk along the road, when you lie down and when you get up.
Tie them as reminders on your hands and bind them on your foreheads.
Write them on the doorposts of your houses and on your gates.
And when the LORD your God brings you into the land He swore to your fathers, to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, that He would give you, a land with great and splendid cities that you did not build,
with houses full of every good thing with which you did not fill them, with wells that you did not dig, and with vineyards and olive groves that you did not plant, and when you eat and are satisfied,
be careful not to forget the LORD who brought you out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of slavery.
Fear the LORD your God, serve Him only, and take your oaths in His name.
Do not follow other gods, the gods of the peoples around you.
For the LORD your God, who is among you, is a jealous God. Otherwise the anger of the LORD your God will be kindled against you, and He will wipe you off the face of the earth.
Do not {{nasah|test}} the LORD your God as you tested Him at Massah.
You are to diligently keep the commandments of the LORD your God and the testimonies and statutes He has given you.
Do what is right and good in the sight of the LORD, so that it may be well with you and that you may enter and possess the good land that the LORD your God swore to give your fathers,
driving out all your enemies before you, as the LORD has said.
In the future, when your son asks, “What is the meaning of the decrees and statutes and ordinances that the LORD our God has commanded you?”
then you are to tell him, “We were slaves of Pharaoh in Egypt, but the LORD brought us out of Egypt with a mighty hand.
Before our eyes the LORD inflicted great and devastating signs and wonders on Egypt, on Pharaoh, and on all his household.
But He brought us out from there to lead us in and give us the land that He had sworn to our fathers.
And the LORD commanded us to observe all these statutes and to fear the LORD our God, that we may always be prosperous and preserved, as we are to this day.
And if we are careful to observe every one of these commandments before the LORD our God, as He has commanded us, then that will be our righteousness.”

shema.gloss: Hear, listen, and by implication obey. In Hebrew thought, truly hearing always includes responding in action; this single word gives its name to the whole confession that follows, and to the daily prayer built from this passage.

echad.gloss: One, unique, undivided. Declares the LORD's exclusive uniqueness against every rival god, and can also carry a sense of composite unity, as in 'one flesh' (Genesis 2:24), language the New Testament later develops in describing the one God known as Father, Son, and Spirit.

ahav.gloss: To love. In covenant contexts this is not primarily an emotion but a loyal, active devotion, the same word used of covenant love between a suzerain and vassal, demanding whole-person allegiance expressed in obedience.

nasah.gloss: To test, to try, to put to the proof. Used of Israel demanding proof of God's presence at Massah rather than trusting what He had already shown them; the word describes presumption rather than legitimate faith seeking understanding.

basic.context: This chapter contains the Shema, Israel's foundational confession that the LORD alone is God, and the command to love Him with everything, heart, soul, and strength, teaching it continually to the next generation as they enter a land of abundance that could easily make them forget Him.
basic.hermeneutics: This passage is both confession and command for Israel's specific covenant life, and Jesus Himself lifts its core straight into the New Testament as the greatest commandment of all, showing its truth was never merely bound to old-covenant Israel but expresses God's unchanging claim on every person's whole heart.
basic.originalLanguages: Shema, hear, implies hearing that results in obedience, not passive listening. Echad, one, declares the LORD's exclusive uniqueness, and the New Testament later finds in this same language room for the one God to be known as Father, Son, and Spirit. Ahav, love, describes loyal, active devotion, not mere feeling, and Israel is warned against nasah, testing God presumptuously rather than trusting Him.
basic.typology: Israel's call to teach these words continually to their children anticipates the New Testament's call for households to raise children in the instruction of the Lord, Ephesians 6:4, while the whole-hearted love commanded here finds its only perfect fulfillment in Christ, the one person who has ever loved God with total, undivided devotion.
basic.christ: When asked for the greatest commandment, Jesus quotes Deuteronomy 6:4-5 directly, Mark 12:29-30, naming it first and foundational, with love of neighbor as its necessary companion. Christ alone fulfills this command perfectly on behalf of His people, and by His Spirit He begins to write this same love into believers' hearts.
basic.god: God is one, utterly unique, tolerating no rival, yet He is also intensely relational, desiring not mere external compliance but total, wholehearted love; He warns against forgetting Him in prosperity precisely because He knows comfort more easily displaces devotion than hardship does.
basic.crossReferences: Mark 12:29-30 records Jesus naming this passage the greatest commandment. Matthew 4:7 shows Jesus refusing to test God, quoting Deuteronomy 6:16 against the devil. Ephesians 6:4 echoes the call to teach children diligently. Romans 10:5-13 reflects on righteousness through faith rather than mere legal observance.
basic.oneStory: This is the theological heartbeat of the whole Old Testament: one God, deserving total love, to be taught continually to every generation. Jesus confirms it as the greatest commandment and, by living it perfectly and giving His Spirit, makes it possible for His people to actually begin to love God as this chapter commands.

context.literary: This chapter follows directly from the restated Ten Commandments in chapter 5, expanding the first and greatest of them, exclusive devotion to the LORD, into the sweeping, foundational call of the Shema before turning to warnings about prosperity, testing God, and teaching children.
context.historical: Addressed to a generation about to inherit an already-developed land, full of houses, wells, and vineyards they did not build, Moses anticipates the specific spiritual danger prosperity poses: comfortable people forget the God who delivered them from slavery far more easily than desperate people do.
context.canonical: The Shema became, and remains, the central daily confession of Jewish faith, recited morning and evening; its citation by Jesus as the greatest commandment cements its place as the theological center not only of Deuteronomy but of the whole Bible's ethical vision.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To call Israel to undivided, wholehearted devotion to the one true God, and to establish a pattern of continual, ordinary-life teaching so that this devotion would survive contact with a prosperous, settled land.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The call to exclusive, wholehearted love for God and the pattern of teaching it continually to children are directly prescriptive and abiding, since they flow from who God is, not merely from Israel's specific national situation. The precise cultural expressions, such as binding words on hands and doorposts, may be read literally or symbolically, but the underlying command to saturate ordinary life with devotion to God carries forward.

debates.question: Does 'the LORD is one' (echad) in verse 4 primarily assert numerical uniqueness or something more like composite unity?

views.label: Echad as strict, exclusive uniqueness
views.case: In context, the emphasis is polemical against the many gods of the surrounding nations: Israel's God is one, not one among many, demanding undivided loyalty rather than the divided worship common in the ancient world.

views.label: Echad as compatible with a later, fuller unfolding of God's nature
views.case: The same Hebrew word describes a man and wife becoming 'one flesh' in Genesis 2:24, a unity of more than one; some see this as leaving conceptual room for the New Testament's fuller revelation of the one God eternally existing as Father, Son, and Spirit, without at all suggesting Moses himself intended a Trinitarian statement.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the verse's driving force is exclusive devotion to the one true God against every rival; the difference concerns what later revelation may legitimately be seen developing from, rather than reading back into, this text.

study.originalLanguages: Shema calls Israel not merely to listen but to obey; echad declares the LORD's exclusive, undivided uniqueness; ahav demands loyal, active love with heart, soul, and strength, the whole person; and nasah warns against testing God presumptuously, as Israel had already done at Massah, rather than resting in trust of what He had already shown them.

typology.type: Israel's call to teach God's words continually to their children
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the New Testament household pattern of raising children in the instruction of the Lord, Ephesians 6:4.

typology.type: The command to love God with all heart, soul, and strength
typology.fulfillment: Perfectly fulfilled only in Christ, who alone has loved the Father with total, undivided devotion, and who by His Spirit begins this same love in His people.

study.christ: Asked which commandment is greatest, Jesus answers by quoting Deuteronomy 6:4-5 word for word, then pairs it immediately with love of neighbor, Mark 12:29-31, showing that the entire law and prophets hang on these two commands. Christ Himself is the only person who has ever perfectly loved God with all His heart, soul, and strength, and He now, by His Spirit, begins to write this same love onto the hearts of those who trust Him, Romans 5:5.
study.god: God is echad, utterly one and unique, tolerating no divided loyalty, and He is also deeply relational, longing not for mere outward compliance but for total, wholehearted love from His people; He warns specifically against the numbing effect of prosperity because He knows comfort dulls devotion more subtly than hardship ever could.

crossReferences.note: Jesus quotes Deuteronomy 6:4-5 directly, naming it the greatest commandment and pairing it with love of neighbor.
Jesus refuses to test God, quoting Deuteronomy 6:16 directly against the devil's temptation.
Echoes the call to raise children continually in the teaching and instruction of the Lord.
God's love poured into believers' hearts by the Spirit, enabling the very love this chapter commands.

study.oneStory: This is the theological heartbeat of the whole Old Testament: one God, deserving total love, taught continually to every generation so that prosperity never displaces devotion. Jesus confirms it as the greatest commandment, and by fulfilling it perfectly and pouring out His Spirit, He makes it possible for His people to actually begin loving God as this chapter has always demanded.

sermon.title: Knowing God
sermon.why: Keller preaches directly through the Shema and its call to wholehearted love, showing how knowing God rightly, not mere religious duty, is what this chapter and the greatest commandment are actually asking for.

quiz.q: What is the significance of Jesus quoting Deuteronomy 6:4-5 in the Gospels?

options.text: He named it the greatest commandment, foundational for every other command including love of neighbor.
options.note: Mark 12:29-31 records Jesus quoting this passage and naming it first and greatest.

options.text: He rejected it as an outdated rule no longer binding.
options.note: Jesus affirms it as the greatest commandment, the opposite of rejecting it.

options.text: He said it applied only to a future generation of Israel.
options.note: Jesus applies it as a present, universal command, not a future-only one.

options.text: He never referenced this passage.
options.note: Mark 12:29-31 records Jesus quoting it directly.

quiz.q: What does verse 4 mean by declaring 'the LORD is One'?

options.text: It declares the LORD's exclusive, undivided uniqueness; there is no other god to divide Israel's loyalty.
options.note: In its context this functions as a polemical declaration of God's uniqueness against the many gods of the nations.

options.text: It means Israel worships exactly one idol among several available options.
options.note: The verse rules out worship of any other god at all, not a choice among several.

options.text: It means God is an impersonal force rather than a personal being.
options.note: The whole passage addresses God relationally, as one to be loved with heart, soul, and strength, not an impersonal force.

options.text: It has no bearing on how Israel is to worship.
options.note: Verse 5 immediately draws out its direct bearing on how Israel is to love and worship God.

quiz.q: How are parents instructed to teach God's words to their children (vv.6-9)?

options.text: Continually, in ordinary daily moments at home and on the road, and marked visibly as reminders.
options.note: Verses 6-9 describe teaching woven into everyday rhythms of life, not confined to a single formal setting.

options.text: Only once a year at a single festival.
options.note: The text describes constant, daily teaching, not an annual event.

options.text: Only through professional priests.
options.note: The command is addressed directly to parents, not delegated to priests.

options.text: Children are not to be taught these words at all.
options.note: Verses 7 and 20-25 give extensive instruction for teaching children specifically.

quiz.q: What warning does Moses give about the good land Israel is about to receive (vv.10-12)?

options.text: That when they enjoy houses, wells, and vineyards they did not build, they must be careful not to forget the LORD.
options.note: Verses 10-12 give this exact warning about the danger prosperity poses to remembering God.

options.text: That the land would be barren and unproductive.
options.note: The land is described as full of abundance, the opposite of barren.

options.text: That they must never build any houses in the land.
options.note: The warning concerns forgetting God amid abundance, not a prohibition on building.

options.text: No warning at all is given about the land.
options.note: Verses 10-12 give an explicit, pointed warning.

quiz.q: What does Moses forbid in verse 16 regarding testing the LORD?

options.text: Testing Him as Israel did at Massah, presuming on God rather than trusting Him.
options.note: Verse 16 explicitly references the incident at Massah as the pattern to avoid.

options.text: Asking God any questions at all in prayer.
options.note: The command addresses presumptuous testing, not honest questioning or prayer.

options.text: Building altars in His honor.
options.note: Altar-building is not addressed in this verse at all.

options.text: Nothing is forbidden here regarding testing God.
options.note: Verse 16 gives an explicit prohibition.

questions: Jesus calls this the greatest commandment. Is loving God with all your heart, soul, and strength actually the animating center of your life, or has something else quietly taken first place?

Moses commands that God's words be taught continually, woven into ordinary moments of daily life. Where do conversations about God happen naturally in your home and relationships, and where are they conspicuously absent?

Moses warns that prosperity itself, houses you did not build, blessings you did not earn, is one of the greatest threats to remembering God. How is comfort or abundance currently shaping your sense of dependence on Him?
Israel tested God at Massah by demanding proof rather than trusting His track record. Where are you tempted to demand a new sign from God rather than trusting what He has already shown you?
The Shema calls for love that involves heart, soul, and strength, the whole person. Which part of yourself do you find hardest to actually give over to God right now?

translationNotes.note: The Hebrew can be rendered 'the LORD is one' or 'the LORD alone', and both readings agree on the verse's central point, exclusive devotion to the one true God against every rival.
'Love' (ahav) in covenant contexts describes loyal, active devotion expressed in obedience, not primarily an emotional feeling, though it certainly includes the heart.
'As you tested Him at Massah' refers back to the incident in Exodus 17:1-7, when Israel demanded proof of God's presence rather than trusting what He had already shown them.
